title: SEGMENT 8 — Cerebral organoids: Lancaster, Knoblich, and the mini-brain

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — Cerebral organoids: Lancaster, Knoblich, and the mini-brain
We come now to the organoid system that, more than any other, has captured the public imagination — and raised the deepest ethical questions.
The cerebral organoid.
The mini-brain.
The seminal paper is from 2013, from the lab of Jürgen Knoblich at the Institute of Molecular Biotechnology in Vienna.
The first author was a postdoc named Madeline Lancaster, who has since become a major figure in the field in her own right.
Their paper, in Nature, was titled "Cerebral organoids model human brain development and microcephaly.
" It introduced a protocol that, with refinements, is essentially the standard cerebral organoid method today.
The recipe is roughly this.
You start with human pluripotent stem cells — embryonic stem cells or iPSCs.
You aggregate them into small spheroids called embryoid bodies, in low-attachment plates so they don't stick to the bottom and stay rounded.

You expose them to a defined sequence of media: first, conditions that push them toward neuroectoderm (the embryonic precursor of the central nervous system); then, conditions that favor expansion of neural progenitors.

After about a week, you embed the aggregates in Matrigel droplets — this is the moment they have the substrate to start building three-dimensional architecture — and then transfer them to a spinning bioreactor, which keeps the medium swirling around the organoids and improves nutrient and oxygen delivery to the interior.
Over the next several weeks, something remarkable happens.
The organoids grow, ballooning outward in irregular shapes.
As they grow, they develop distinct regions — clusters of cells expressing markers of specific brain regions.
Forebrain regions.
Hindbrain regions.
Some optic-cup-like structures.

And critically, within the forebrain regions, the cells organize into structures that resemble the developing cortex: a layer of neural progenitors lining a fluid-filled cavity (resembling a ventricle), with newly born neurons migrating outward from that progenitor zone toward the outside of the organoid.
The cells produce neurons of multiple cortical layer identities — early-born deep-layer neurons first, later-born superficial-layer neurons later, in the right order.
They produce intermediate progenitor cells and early glia.
After two months in culture, you can record electrical activity from individual neurons; after six months, you can see early forms of neural network activity.
The Lancaster paper used cerebral organoids to model microcephaly, a developmental disorder in which the brain fails to grow to normal size.

By deriving iPSCs from a patient with a genetic form of microcephaly (caused by mutations in a gene called CDK5RAP2), making cerebral organoids from those iPSCs, and comparing them to organoids from healthy donors, they showed that the patient-derived organoids were significantly smaller, with reduced progenitor expansion and premature neuronal differentiation.
They were modeling, in a dish, the very developmental defect that produces small brains in living patients.
That's a powerful demonstration: human brain development, in its earliest stages, being recapitulated and disrupted on a benchtop, with the disruption revealing the cellular mechanism of a disease.
In the years since, cerebral organoids have proliferated into many variants.
Some are unguided — like Lancaster's original — and produce organoids with a mixture of brain regions in unpredictable proportions.
Some are guided or region-specific — produced with cocktails that bias development toward, say, just cortical tissue, or just thalamic tissue, or just midbrain tissue.
The Sergiu Pasca lab at Stanford has been a leader in developing region-specific organoids, including organoids of cortex, striatum, thalamus, cerebellum, midbrain, and spinal cord.
By choosing which region to grow, researchers can ask precise questions about that region's biology in human cells.
What cerebral organoids recapitulate is remarkable.
Cortical layers.
Ventricle-like spaces.
Progressive neurogenesis with the right developmental timing.
Migration of newborn neurons toward the cortical plate.
The emergence of specific neuronal subtypes.
Functional electrical activity, including action potentials and, eventually, synaptic communication.
Late-stage organoids — kept in culture for many months — show early forms of network synchronization and oscillatory activity that resemble the kind of activity seen in fetal brain.
What they don't recapitulate is also notable.
They don't have proper vasculature, so they max out at a few millimeters and the interior becomes hypoxic and necrotic.
They don't have microglia, the brain's resident immune cells, unless you co-culture them in.
They don't have the full repertoire of subcortical structures connected to cortex.
They don't have the precise lamination of a mature cortex.
And — this is important — they don't reach the developmental maturity of an adult brain.
Most current cerebral organoids correspond to mid-fetal human brain at best, around 15 to 25 weeks of gestation.
They are early.
They are immature.
They are, biologically, fetal tissue.
We'll talk about the ethical implications of cerebral organoids in Segment 13.

For now, hold this thought: a piece of human brain tissue, with electrical activity, with cortical layers, with developmental programs running — in a dish, on a benchtop, accessible to experimentation in ways no living human brain ever could be.
This is an unprecedented capability, and it forces unprecedented questions.
